violations.detail: Back is 14 words: 'Every request receives a non-error response (without guarantee it's the most recent write)'. The parenthetical adds a new fact (what's NOT guaranteed), which should be a separate card or removed.
Back is 14 words: 'The system continues to operate despite messages being dropped or delayed between nodes'. Trim to something like 'The system operates despite network message loss or delay'.
Back is 17 words. Should be trimmed, e.g. 'At most two of: consistency, availability, partition tolerance'.
Back is 13 words: 'Network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, so you must tolerate them'. Trim to e.g. 'Network partitions are inevitable'.

violations.rule: Atomicity — back has two facts joined by dash-elaboration
violations.detail: Back is 'Availability — it may refuse requests to preserve consistency'. The dash adds elaboration which is a second fact. Should be just 'Availability'.

violations.rule: Atomicity — back has two facts joined by dash-elaboration
violations.detail: Back is 'Consistency — it may serve stale data to remain available'. Same dash-elaboration issue. Should be just 'Consistency'.

If you know a CP system sacrifices availability, then an AP system sacrificing consistency is trivially deducible (and vice versa). These are mirror-deducible. Keep one or merge into a single multi-cloze card.

The cloze 'Cassandra is classified as an {{c1::AP}} system in the CAP theorem because it prioritizes availability over consistency.' The answer 'AP' is directly guessable from 'prioritizes availability' in the surrounding text. The context gives away the answer.

violations.rule: Historical trivia — skip unless studying history
violations.detail: Card asks who conjectured the CAP theorem and when it was proved (Eric Brewer, 2000, 2002). This is historical trivia — a practitioner does not need these facts to use or apply the CAP theorem. The SKILL.md and card selection guide explicitly list 'year published, who invented it' as anti-patterns to skip.

violations.rule: No enumerations (even disguised as cloze with 2 items)
violations.detail: Card 13 has two independent cloze fills ({{c1::Eric Brewer}} and {{c2::2002}}) testing distinct facts (who and when). This is a 2-item cloze enumeration — each fill is an independent fact. Even if the card weren't trivia, it would need splitting.
Back is 16 words: 'If no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value'. Trim to e.g. 'All replicas eventually converge to the same value'.

Back is 28 words containing two distinct facts joined by a semicolon: (1) the misconception (permanently choose 2 of 3) and (2) the reality (tradeoff only during partitions). Should be split into separate cards or drastically trimmed.

Of 14 cards: ~4 are definitional (cards 1-3, 11-12), 2 are reasoning/why (5-6), 2 are tradeoff (7-8), 2 are application (9-10), 1 is misconception (14), 1 is trivia (13). The why/how proportion is ~14%, well below the ~40% target. Too many cards are definitional or classification-based.
